mnemonics.title: Type Options Memory
mnemonics.content: TAPF: -t (Type), -a (All), -p (Path), -f (Function skip) - Remember: 'Tony Always Plays Football' to recall option purposes

mnemonics.title: Command Resolution Order
mnemonics.content: Aunt Karen Fought Battles For Love - Alias, Keyword, Function, Builtin, File - Order of shell command resolution

mnemonics.title: Type Output Words
mnemonics.content: A K F B F - First letters of: Alias, Keyword, Function, Builtin, File - the five types identified by type command

acronyms.usage: Remembering that type reveals the 'essence' or nature of a command

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flowchart: Input Command → Check Aliases → Check Keywords → Check Functions → Check Builtins → Check PATH Files → Execute. This shows the priority order.
Create a table with command in left column and -t output in right: cd→builtin, if→keyword, ls→file, myfunc→function, ll→alias
Remember -t as 'tiny output' (one word), -a as 'all' (multiple), -p as 'path' (file location), -f as 'filter' (skip functions)

analogies.analogy: Think of `type` as a detective that investigates a command's identity - asking 'Who are you really?' to aliases pretending to be commands, functions hiding as commands, or files masquerading as builtins.

Commands are like actors: Alias is an impersonator (pretends to be something else), Keyword is a script writer (controls flow), Function is an understudy (custom role), Builtin is the director's assistant (internal), File is an external actor (separate process).

When a command is typed, the shell interviews candidates in order: Alias (first choice), Keyword (interrupts), Function (specialist), Builtin (internal hire), File (external applicant). The first qualified candidate gets the job.
